danger_signals.visual_form: Rival driver vehicle positioning and collision behavior on track
danger_signals.meaning: Indicates hostile/dirty driving tactics; visual indicator of competitive threat level

danger_signals.visual_form: Visual wear on vehicle model, smoke/sparks from engine, transmission malfunction indicators
danger_signals.meaning: Communicates vehicle degradation state and imminent race failure risk

danger_signals.visual_form: Vehicle contact animation, crash sequence, spin-out behavior
danger_signals.meaning: Immediate danger to race position and vehicle integrity

danger_signals.visual_form: Vehicle placement relative to walls and other racers in outside line positioning
danger_signals.meaning: Indicates exposure to forced-off-track scenarios (e.g., being pushed into wall)

safety_signals.visual_form: Pit crew presence/absence UI, crew member model visibility during pit stops
safety_signals.meaning: Indicates mechanical support and repair capability; affects race continuation viability

safety_signals.visual_form: Clean vehicle model appearance, absence of visual damage indicators, engine sound quality
safety_signals.meaning: Signals mechanical safety and performance readiness

safety_signals.visual_form: Vehicle placement at front of race field, clear track ahead
safety_signals.meaning: Communicates maximum control state and reduced collision risk

safety_signals.visual_form: Pit crew animations completing repairs, vehicle departure from pit lane with fresh engine/parts
safety_signals.meaning: Indicates recovery from mechanical failure and restored race viability

faction_indicators.color_identity: Primary team car livery (No. 46 City Chevrolet, No. 51 Mello Yello Chevrolet)
faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Car number on vehicle hull and pit crew uniform identity
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Rival team vehicles display different paint schemes and sponsor logos (Rowdy Burns' No. 51 pre-rivalry, Russ Wheeler team vehicle)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Pit crew positioning and uniform colors match player car livery; victory lane celebration zone for player faction

faction_indicators.color_identity: Winston Cup Champion vehicle livery, distinct from player car scheme
faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Rowdy Burns car number and team sponsor branding
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Initially displays aggressive on-track behavior visuals; post-reconciliation shows collaborative pit crew dynamics
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Daytona race venue; hospital setting for character interaction (temporary faction neutralization during recovery)

faction_indicators.color_identity: Secondary team livery under Daland ownership, visually distinct vehicle scheme
faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Russ Wheeler car number, Daland team branding
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Displays maximum aggression visual cues: wall-forcing maneuvers, pit lane blocking animations, post-race confrontation sequences
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Multiple race venues (North Wilkesboro, Atlanta); Daland's dealership/team headquarters as faction headquarters location

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Vehicle model shows frequent collision damage, erratic positioning on track, multiple DNF (did-not-finish) sequences
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: AI rivals display dominant positioning, player car struggles with handling cues, pit crew appears less coordinated

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Mid-season - Cole's training completion and rivalry escalation
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Vehicle model becomes cleaner, race positioning improves, slingshot maneuver animation introduced as victory signature
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Rowdy Burns displays competitive-but-friendly positioning; Russ Wheeler escalates to dirty driving visuals

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Injury recovery phase - player temporary absence
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Hospital/medical facility environment replaces race track; vehicle absent from action; Russ Wheeler car takes lead position in races
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Cole character shown in recovery state; pit crew works for Russ instead; Daland shows favor to Russ faction visually

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Pit crew fragmentation visual; Harry and Cole removed from Daland team colors; vehicle reassignment to Rowdy Burns' car
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Post-race fight animation; pit crew confrontation sequences; Daland appears as antagonist in pit area

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Combined pit crew collaboration visual (Harry + Daland crews working same vehicle); engine swap animation sequence
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Daland aids Cole despite earlier dismissal; Russ displays predictable maneuver (expecting outside slingshot); Cole's crossover inside-pass animation as climactic maneuver

environmental_storytelling.technique: Pit crew visual hierarchy and accessibility
environmental_storytelling.example: Pit crew absence when Daland fires Cole/Harry communicates team dissolution; crew reappearance shows reinstatement or temporary alliance

environmental_storytelling.technique: Vehicle damage accumulation as narrative marker
environmental_storytelling.example: Visible dents/paint damage on Cole's car increases through early races, reset by pit stop repairs, communicates learning curve progression

environmental_storytelling.technique: Track location significance in character relationships
environmental_storytelling.example: Daytona venue associates with injury/reconciliation turning points; Daytona 500 final race location emphasizes championship redemption arc

Rowdy Burns' initial aggressive inside-line positioning shifts to collaborative spacing after character reconciliation; Russ Wheeler maintains wall-forcing behavior throughout, visually communicating unchanging antagonism
Final race victory shows both Harry and Claire present, pit crew celebration inclusion, communicates complete social restoration vs. earlier isolated victories

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Frequent spinouts, late braking animations, collision-prone track positioning; slingshot maneuver not yet available in arsenal
Smooth line navigation, signature slingshot maneuver execution animations, winning race sequences
Vehicle absent from track; character shown in medical setting instead of race environment
Inside-line aggressive positioning, early-season collision attempts; visual display of Winston Cup Champion confidence
Defensive positioning becomes supportive; requests Cole drive his car; pit crew collaboration visible in final race sequence
Consistent wall-forcing positioning, pit lane blocking maneuvers, post-race collision retribution sequences; no visible state change throughout season
Provides resources to Cole's car; pit crew availability; engine provision during final race despite earlier dismissal
Favor visual shift toward Russ Wheeler; dismissal animation of Cole/Harry; competitive defense of newest driver resource allocation
Pit crew direction animations, mechanical diagnosis (metal in oil pan detection), pit lane strategy execution
Absence from pit area during Russ races; visual restoration to pit crew leadership in final race

notes: Days of Thunder NES is a sports-racing title based on 1990 film IP. Sources provided are disambiguation and film context only, with no detailed game mechanics documentation available. Manifestation patterns extracted are inferred from film narrative structure, which likely influenced game world-state progression and visual faction indicators. The game uses vehicle livery, pit crew presence/absence, damage accumulation, and character positioning as primary world-state communication methods. Rivalry escalation and team dynamics drive visual/behavioral changes more prominently than traditional danger/safety signaling systems. The film's focus on interpersonal rivalry (Cole vs. Rowdy, Cole vs. Russ) suggests game manifests social/political tension through opponent behavior patterns and pit crew allocation visibility rather than abstract 'danger' indicators. Data tier T3 assessment appropriate given reliance on narrative context rather than empirical game footage analysis.
